result:
I want to write about the moment the sky rips open and no one notices.  
Not a thunderclap, not a cinematic alien portal—just a soft, almost courteous tear at the seam between here and everything else, like silk deciding it no longer wishes to be a scarf.  
The edges curl back without a sound, revealing a second sky behind the first, dimmer, older, the color of letters left too long in a drawer.  
People keep walking their dogs, checking their phones, inhaling the sour steam from coffee lids.  
A pigeon keeps pebbling the sidewalk for bagel shards.  
The tear widens, forming an ellipse the length of a city bus, and still no neck cranes.  
It is not invisible; it is only uninteresting, the way rain is uninteresting to fish.  

I want to write about the quiet policy the universe enacted in 1348, when half of Europe coughed itself into graves.  
A bureaucrat angel with ink-stained wings stamped the form:  
“Going forward, cataclysms shall be scaled to the individual.  
Plagues will be blood-borne heartbreaks.  
Wars will be fought one cancelled birthday at a time.”  
Since then, the sky has ripped open daily—  
in laundromats at 2 a.m. when every dryer sighs at once,  
in the pause between a physician’s sentence and its translation into fear,  
in the parking lot where you sit after closing the trunk on the last box that used to belong to both of you.  
The tear spreads just wide enough for one person to slip through, or for the smell of forsythia to slip out.  
No collective gasp, no newspaper typeface large enough.  
History books keep counting armies, hurricanes, shares of stock.  
Meanwhile, the policy hums along, a cosmic sewing machine stitching private apocalypses into hemlines no one alters.

I want to write about the childhood pact I signed with the yellow jacket that landed on my wrist at age seven.  
I promised not to swat if she promised to tell me what the sun tastes like.  
She walked across the freckles of my arm, each foot a hot needle,  
then lifted off, leaving only the echo of her wings—  
a tiny exhaust fan cooling the moment.  
I kept my end; I never learned the flavor of sunlight.  
But every summer now, when the first yellow jacket cruises the picnic table like a drunk bidder at an estate sale,  
I feel the pact renew.  
I sit very still, lemonade sweating in my hand,  
offering trespass, receiving nothing,  
a monk of almosts.

I want to write about the municipal museum of unfinished gestures.  
It is open Tuesdays and whenever you hear a kettle begin to whistle then think better of it.  
Exhibit A: the hand that once reached to tuck a strand of hair behind an ear, aborted mid-air when the couple realized they were no longer lovers, just evidence.  
The hand hovers in formaldehyde light, fingers still curved like parentheses around an absent face.  
Docents invite you to place your own palm against the glass; the ghost condensation of the hand aligns with yours, warm as regret.  
In the gift shop you can buy snow globes that contain only the first flake, watches whose minute hands stutter forward then retreat like shy metronomes.  
At closing time the lights do not dim; instead they intensify until every shadow is erased, proving that absence can be overexposed too.

I want to write about the night my grandmother’s radio began to speak in her own voice.  
She had died three weeks earlier.  
The appliance was an off-white Sanyo, circa 1978, its left speaker cracked during an argument about whether Johnny Cash was sexy.  
At 3:12 a.m. it clicked on by itself, volume knob crawling like a clock hand reversing arthritis.  
First came the usual static, the cosmic microwave background rewarmed.  
Then the voice—not a memory, not a recording—offered the weather report for a date thirteen years in the future.  
Chance of lilac rain, high of every temperature you forgot to notice.  
She advised the living to plant cucumbers earlier, to mistrust any recipe requiring exactly one teaspoon of faith.  
My mother stood in her nightgown, arms wrapped around herself like a straitjacket made of genealogy,  
listening to her mother forecast storms that would arrive after her own bones had joined the choir invisible.  
When the voice signed off—“and that’s all the news that fits in the afterlife, folks”—the radio snapped to a commercial for mattresses you cannot die on.  
We never figured out whether the message was comfort or depreciation schedule.  
The Sanyo still works; we keep it in the attic, unplugged, like a bee in a jar with no airholes, hoping it will choose to stay.

I want to write about the taxonomy of small extinguishings.  
Kingdom: Almost.  
Phylum: Could Have.  
Class: Too Late.  
Order: The sound a tunnel makes when you exit and your ears forget to pop.  
Family: Coffee rings on library books—brown galaxies orbiting a sentence you will never finish.  
Genus: The extra heartbeat you feel when descending stairs in the dark, the one that doesn’t belong to your body.  
Species: The specific flicker of the bedside alarm clock when it changes from 2:59 to 2:60—yes, 2:60—before correcting itself,  
a hiccup in chronometry, a private leap second inserted by a sleepy god who miscounted.  
Natural habitat: the pause after you ask “Are you still there?” on a call that shows full bars.  
Conservation status: critically endangered, yet reproducing faster than we can record the losses.

I want to write about the man who collected every version of himself that never happened.  
He kept them in mason jars on shelves that sagged like disappointed shoulders.  
Here: the him who learned to tango at sixty, feet still tender from decades of marching in place.  
Here: the him who said yes to the woman painting seagulls on china plates at the flea market, the one who offered him a life smelling of turpentine and salt.  
Each jar glowed a slightly different hue, like supermarket juices no one buys—guava-mango, starfruit-lime, the color of alternate Tuesdays.  
At night he unscrewed a lid, tipped the almost-him onto the bedroom floor; it unfolded like a nylon tent, transparent but tall enough to bump the ceiling.  
They spoke in a language constructed entirely of conditional verbs.  
When morning came, the tent collapsed back into the jar, leaving only a salty ring, the way tears outline eyes after crying in a dream you swear you didn’t have.  
One day the shelf finally broke.  
Jars rolled, glass kissed tile, possibilities evacuated.  
The apartment filled with a thousand versions of one man, all talking at once, a chorus of could-have-beens harmonizing into white noise.  
Neighbors banged on walls; the landlord threatened eviction; but the man just stood in the middle, bathed in fluorescent futures,  
realizing for the first time that absence can be plural, that silence itself can crowd a room until you cannot breathe for the weight of every word you never spoke.

I want to write about the exact weight of the sentence “I forgive you.”  
On a digital scale it registers 0.2 grams, the mass of a single lash fallen from the eye of someone who stayed awake all night rehearsing.  
But carried, it outweighs cathedrals.  
People trudge across continents with it chained to the ankle, unaware they possess the key between their molars.  
Sometimes the sentence mutates, becomes “I forgive you (but).”  
The parenthetical weighs 2.2 tons and hangs from the ribcage like a bell no one rings because the clapper is shaped like your own face.  
Entire civilizations have fallen because a messenger mispronounced the accent, turning forgiveness into foreknowledge, a prophecy of repeated wounds.  
Linguists insist the sentence is intransitive; it needs no object to be true.  
Tell that to the woman who whispers it nightly to the empty side of a bed still dented like snow that remembers the deer.

I want to write about the sky again, the rip I started with.  
By now it has grown ragged, a celestial flag used too many years in too many wars.  
Through it you can see the backstage of the cosmos: rope lights, sandbags, the prompt book of physics left open to the scene where gravity forgets its lines.  
Stagehands in black turtlenecks move scrims of stars into new configurations—tonight Orion wears a belt of neon叹signs advertising vacancies in the heart.  
No one on Earth looks up; they are busy photographing their lunches, arguing about buses, teaching toddlers to blow soap bubbles shaped like extinct animals.  
The tear itself begins to tire.  
It sighs, a sound like a zipper changing its mind, and starts to heal, threads of probable blue pulling across the wound.  
Within minutes the sky is seamless again, a lid screwed tight on a jar labeled “Tomorrow, cloudy with a chance of amnesia.”  
But somewhere, in the fraction of a second before the rip closes, a single photon of the older, dimmer sky escapes.  
It travels 93 million miles to reach the back of your eye,  
arriving just as you glance away from your phone,  
just as you notice the yellow jacket circling your glass,  
just as you remember you never tasted the sun.  
The photon lands on your retina like the soft, almost courteous tear of silk deciding it no longer wishes to be a scarf.  
You blink.  
The world looks ordinary, but for the rest of the day everything you see carries, just beneath its surface, the faint afterimage of a sky that was tired of pretending to be the only one.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
